content.full_text:
7

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 11, 1993

Kathryn Donovan
Law Offices of Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
400 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-219

Dear Ms. Donovan:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District
("BART"), AAI Corporation of Maryland ("AAI") and America
Gateway Corporation of Pennsylvania ("AGC") regarding their
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act of 1974, as amended (the "Act"). Specifically, you
ask whether the McEwen Advice Letter, No. I-92-481 applies to
individuals at AAI/AGC who will be providing professional services
to BART in Phase 1 of the suspended light rail technology project
(the "SLRT Project").

QUESTION

Whether individuals at AAI/AGC, who qualify as "consultants"
within the meaning of the Act, may assist BART in preparation of
the feasibility study required by Phase 1 of the SLRT Project, if
it is reasonably foreseeable that AAI/AGC will be selected to
perform additional services in Phases 2 and/or 3 of the same
project, should BART win the federal competition and elect to
proceed with the project?

CONCLUSION

Individuals at AAI/AGC may participate in agency decisions in
Phase 1 of the project despite the fact these decisions will
affect their employers' future agreements with BART for Phase 2
and/or Phase 3 of the project (and thus have a material financial
effect on their employers), since the decision to retain AAI/AGC
for all three phases of the SLRT project has already been made by
BART.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-219
Page 2


FACTS

In the summer of 1992, BART entered a competition sponsored
by the Federal Transit Administration ("FTA")² and was selected on
October 20, 1992, as one of three agencies to receive funds for
the purpose of studying construction of a suspended light rail
system..

The SLRT Project envisioned by the federal legislation
consists of three phases:

Phase 1.  Feasibility Study.

Phase 2.  Environmental Documentation and Preliminary
Engineering.

Phase 3.  Construction of the System.

As one of the three selected agencies, BART is now required
to undertake the feasibility study contemplated in Phase 1 of the
SLRT Project.  The study will demonstrate the feasibility of
suspended light rail technology and how it would be applied to
the Oakland Airport site.  This first phase of the project,
BART has been awarded a federal grant in the amount of $333,330
and must provide a 20 percent match in the amount of $83,330.

As part of this competition, the FTA required all applicant
agencies to identify a system vendor (manufacturer/designer) which
could develop a light rail system with specific technological
applications for wheels, motors, etc.  After a review of
transportation publications and consultation with others in the
transit field, staff selected as the system vendor AAI, which, in
conjunction with AGC, had submitted to BART a description of its
proposed technology identical to that described in the Federal
Register announcement.  As required by the existing legislation,
BART entered into a Cooperative Agreement with AAI/AGC.³

_______________
2  The competition was authorized by the Intermodal Surface
Transportation Efficiency Act of 1991 (ISTEA) (Pub. L. 102-240)
and 57 Fed. Reg. 11646.

3  In your letter, you refer to the system vendor for the project
as "AAI/AGC."  In actuality, you state that AGC is the entity
which has developed the technology necessary for the SLRT Project
and that AAI is the entity which would play the lead role in
design and construction of the SLRT Project, using AGC's
technology.  The cooperative agreement executed in July 1992, was
between BART and AGC and, for purposes of the federal competition,
BART specified AAI as the system vendor, based upon an agreement
between AAI/AGC to work jointly with BART on the SLRT Project.
Thus, participation by both AAI/AGC in the SLRT Project is an
integral part of BART's application for federal funding.

Our File No. A-93-219
Page 3


agreement provided, in part, that BART and AAI/AGC would work
together to negotiate the scope and terms of further cooperation
for completion of the first phase of the SLRT Project. It also
contemplated that BART and AAI/AGC would negotiate the scope and
terms of further cooperation for Phases 2 and 3, should BART be
selected and decide, at its sole discretion, to proceed with those
subsequent stages of the project.

Following FTA review of the completed feasibility studies
performed by each of the three competing agencies, FTA will select
one agency to receive an award of $4 million for Phase 2
(environmental documentation and preliminary engineering). If,
after Phase 2, the selected local agency determines to proceed to
construction, and if FTA approves the project, the agency will
then receive at least $30 million to construct the contemplated
SLRT system.

The federal legislation anticipates that, should BART be
selected to proceed with Phases 2 and/or 3 of the SLRT Project,
AAI/AGC would continue to serve in the role of system vendor on
the project. The FTA has interpreted the legislation accordingly;
specifically, the FTA has stated that participation in the SLRT
Project competition requires BART to establish and maintain a
cooperative agreement with a system vendor. The FTA has further
stated that the vendor's technical approach and capacity were
important evaluation factors in the competition the FTA conducted
before awarding grants for Phase 1 of the SLRT Project. BART's
proposed agreement with AAI/AGC for performance of Phase 1 also
will reflect this anticipation of a continuing relationship. It
is planned that the agreement will provide that BART and AAI/AGC
agree to negotiate the scope and terms of further cooperation for
Phases 2 and 3, provided that BART is selected by FTA to proceed
to those phases and, provided further, that BART also determines
to proceed with the SLRT Project. This proposed language would be
similar to the provisions of the cooperative agreement, discussed
above.

For purposes of this letter, you have asked us to assume that
certain individuals at AAI/AGC⁴ who will provide the services
necessary to complete Phase 1 of the SLRT Project will be
"consultants" who are deemed "public officials" subject to the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the act.

ANALYSIS

The primary issue addressed in the McEwen Advice Letter, No.
I-92-481, was whether a consultant has a financial interest in a
governmental decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if, as a

_______________
4 Individuals at both AAI and AGC will provide services on the
SLRT Project. The services may be performed by individuals at
each company working independently of the other company, or by
individuals of the two companies working together.

Our File No. A-93-219
Page 4


result of agency decisions in which the consultant participates.
the consultant's employer would be asked, directed, or required to
perform additional services for which the employer would receive
additional income.

Normally, when a consultant participates in making a
governmental decision that has a foreseeable material financial
effect on a source of income to the consultant (i.e., the
consultant's private employer), the official has a conflict of
interest under the Act. (Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454.) The
McEwen letter provides that not all of these situations will
result in a conflict of interest.

For example, where a governmental entity has already
contracted to permit the consultant to make recommendations that
result in the rendering of identified services for an agreed upon
price, there is no conflict of interest. In this case, the
consultant's participation in governmental decisions will not have
a foreseeable financial effect on the consultant's employer. This
is because, according to the McEwen advice letter, the agency's
decision to pay the consultant's employer for the additional
services contemplated by the contract was previously made by
disinterested agency officials and the consultant's participation
merely constitutes the implementation of that preexisting
decision.

However, where a consultant makes a recommendation to a
public agency that will create additional work and income for the
consultant's employer that is beyond the scope of the contract
under which the consultant is rendering advice, then a conflict of
interest arises. (In re Mahoney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69; Rose
Advice Letter, No. A-84-306.) In this situation, the consultant's
recommendation most likely will have a material financial effect
on the consultant's employer.

Your letter raises essentially two questions concerning the
advice given in the McEwen advice letter:

1. Under the McEwen letter, is it sufficient that a contract
between a consultant's firm and a public agency "contemplates"
future services or does a contract have to specifically enumerate
all the future services to be performed?

2. May the future services contemplated in the "original"
contract be the subject of a separate contract between the same
parties?

Issue 1: The McEwen letter states that a member of a law
firm that qualifies as a consultant under the Act by virtue of a
contract between the attorney's law firm and the governmental
entity may render advice to a public agency, despite the fact that
the attorney's advice will generate additional work and fees for
the attorney's law firm, provided the contract under which the

Our File No. A-93-219
Page 5


attorney is making these recommendations or decisions expressly
provides for payment for these additional services.

"Expressly provides" means that the contract must be definite
and certain as to its terms and requirements. Absolute certainty
is not required; only reasonable certainty, such as will enable
the Commission or the courts to determine the intention of the
parties, if necessary.

Therefore, if the meaning of the contract and the intention
of the parties can with reasonable certainty be ascertained, it is
sufficient for purposes of the McEwen letter that the original
contract contemplates with sufficient specificity subsequent
agreements with the same parties concerning the same discrete
project.

Issue 2: While an agreement to make a future contract is not
a contract, the parties are imposing in their initial contract
conditions precedent,6 the performance of which are essential to
the formation of a second and a third contract. Furthermore,
according to your letter, BART's selection of AAI/AGC as the
system vendor and BART's decision to enter into a cooperative
agreement with AGC, for purposes of the federal competition,
committed BART to a continuing relationship with AAI/AGC for the
duration of BART's participation in the SLRT Project.

Thus, the initial decision by BART officials to select AAI/
AGC to provide professional services in connection with the SLRT
Project has already been made by BART. The fact that the project
has three distinct and divisible phases and that each phase will
be the subject of a separate contract is not determinative to our
inquiry under the unique set of facts presented in your letter.
The significant factor is that BART's decision to retain AAI/AGC
for all three phases of the project is in the parties' "original"
contract.

Therefore, individuals at AAI/AGC may participate in agency
decisions in Phase 1 of the project despite the fact these
decisions will affect their employers' future agreements with BART
for Phase 2 and/or Phase 3 of the project (and thus have a
material financial effect on their employers), since the decision
to retain AAI/AGC for all three phases of the SLRT project has
already been made by BART.

Your situation appears to be virtually indistinguishable from
hypothetical 1 contained in the McEwen letter. Both the
hypothetical and your situation, however, are distinguishable from
the factual circumstances presented in the Mahoney Opinion. In

_______________________
5 BART and AAI/AGC contemplate the AAI/AGC will be asked to
perform additional services in connection with Phases 2 and/or 3
of the project if (1) BART wins the federal competition and (2)
BART elects to proceed with the project.

Our File No. A-93-219
Page 6


Mahoney, the Commission was asked whether a consultant (i.e., the
county surveyor-engineer) could act to approve land levelling and
drainage permits for a job where the consultant's private firm had
contracted to provide surveying services. In concluding that the
consultant could not, the Commission recognized the obvious
potential for bias in that situation. The decisions the
consultant made in his/her official capacity were discretionary
and could have a direct and immediate effect on the amount of work
the consultant's private firm performed in connection with that
permit application, and therefore, the amount of income the
consultant's firm received.

Your situation does not create the compelling potential for
bias found in Mahoney because, under your facts, the decision has
already been made by disinterested BART officials to retain the
services of AAI/AGC for the SLRT Project.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

[signature: Deanne Stone]

By: Deanne Stone
    Senior Commission Counsel




6    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
